Wow, this is news to me.  It honestly doesn't make any sense to me that Python `bytes.decode` works this way.

I mean, look at this:

```
In [7]: 'foo'.encode('UTF-16le')
Out[7]: 'f\x00o\x00o\x00'

In [8]: 'foo'.encode('UTF-16')
Out[8]: '\xff\xfef\x00o\x00o\x00'

In [9]: 'foo'.encode('UTF-16be')
Out[9]: '\x00f\x00o\x00o'
```

Why in the world would you want `UTF-16` to remove the BOM, but not `UTF-16LE` or `UTF-16BE`?  This especially odd when you consider that the BOM-specific codec for UTF-8, `UTF-8-SIG`, removes the BOM whereas the basic version doesn't:

```
In [16]: (codecs.BOM_UTF8 + 'foo'.encode('UTF-8')).decode('utf-8-sig')
Out[16]: u'foo'

In [17]: (codecs.BOM_UTF8 + 'foo'.encode('UTF-8')).decode('utf-8')
Out[17]: u'\ufefffoo'
```

> Out of curiosity, can we get a measurable speedup if you massively increase cache size?

I doubt it.  There are roughly two or three optimizations at play here depending on how you look at it.

1. JSON-fixture tests use many of the same addresses for contracts, transactions, etc.
2. There are a few values that just natively get used a lot like 32 null bytes or empty byte string.
3. There are a few storage values that are going to be common, like lower integers.

The optimizations happen wtih

- `evm.db.hash_trie.HashTrie` which uses `keccak(key)` as the key when storing in the trie.
- `evm.vm.stack` which moves values back and forth between integer and bytes representations (`int_to_big_endian/big_endian_to_int`).
- `evm.db.state` which uses `pad32` for it's storage keys.

I'm going to try bumping the cache sizes.  Maybe `64` was a bit too small and these common values were constantly being evicted.  Will check run-times in the morning and see what things look like.

Assuming that the expected period `period=10`, lookahead length `LOOKAHEAD_LENGTH=4`, period length `PERIOD_LENGTH=5`.

#456 problem became: 
1. When block number is 30 and the length of the collator pool is 10, one collator call `get_eligible_proposer(shard_id=1, period=10)` and get the eligible is the last element (`c9`) of the pool.
2. When block number is 35, `collators[5]` deregisters, so the length of the collator pool becomes 9. Move last element c9 to position 5.

3. One collator call `get_eligible_proposer(shard_id=1, period=10)`:
    1. The length of the collator pool changed..!
    2. Even if we record how many collators join and exit during the lookahead ( https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/456#issuecomment-372551032) to recalculate the population of the collator pool to **a fixed number**, still, since the collator position is changed, we lost the consistency of calling `get_eligible_proposer` function during the lookahead periods.

Your installation is rooted at a depth of 66 characters:

```
echo /share/carvajal-archive/PACKAGES/local/miniconda/miniconda3_perl/ | wc -c
66
```

If you try to install a package built with an 80 character padded prefix (such as Intel's fontconfig) that leaves only 12 characters for the files themselves. Most packages will contain paths consisting of more characters than that.

Do you need to install to such a long prefix? If you were able to use a shorter installation prefix for your root folder then that would prevent this from happening. The shorter the better here.

The best fix for this is that Intel needs to rebuild their `fontconfig` package with `conda-build >=2` and in doing so, ensure that it ends up with a 255 character prefix.

I believe that the problem here isn't with `--prune` necessarily, but with how environments are created with an environment.yml file using conda 4.4.  With conda 4.4, we make the distinction between user-requested specs and specs in an environment that are there to fulfill dependencies of user-requested specs.  By recreating an environment from a typical environment.yml file, you're telling conda that *every* package is a user-requested spec.

The solution going forward is to provide a flag for `conda env export` that allows users to export only user-requested dependencies.

You're really creating and deleting hundreds of indices per day?  How many active indices do you retain on an ongoing basis (per node, the whole cluster)? Each shard, each index has a management cost to the cluster.  If you are creating hundreds of indices per day, and then deleting hundreds of "expired" indices per day, this is a tremendous overhead for an Elasticsearch cluster.  

It may be possible to do what you're requesting, but I'm not sure retooling Curator is the proper solution to this issue.  If you must use a heavy index/shard count per day, you may be better served with a custom script using the Curator API that retries indefinitely until all deletes are completed.  Curator retries 3 times because that's all it should take.  Even that concession was debated hotly at the time it was requested.

Curator is just a specialized API frontend.   Curator sends API requests to Elasticsearch, and Elasticsearch responds.  So why does Curator time out for your use case?  Because Elasticsearch rebalances the cluster by shard count per node by default.  If you're trying to delete so many at once, Elasticsearch is trying to rebalance the cluster.  So many shards and indices shuffling around is taxing your cluster quite heavily, and it is unable to simultaneously rebalance _and_ process new delete (or even create requests, I'd wager) in a timely manner.  The timeouts are an indication of a _very_ busy cluster, as described, and Curator is only responding with what it received from Elasticsearch.

Currently, Curator 4 allows for this feature.  It is in alpha release right now.  I am working to get the first beta out this week, if all goes well.

Curator 4 documentation is at https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/4.0/index.html

Essentially, you'd add extra filters.  Filter stacking is not possible in Curator 3.x, but it is in 4.

Snapshots are not monitored by size in Curator, by the way.  Only indices.

The biggest part is really the index metadata in https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/indexlist.py#L144-L174, where you see that Curator _currently_ uses (this may expand at any time):

* `number_of_replicas`
* `number_of_shards`
* `state`
* `creation_date`
* `routing`

Most of these are under cluster.state['metadata']['indices'][INDEXNAME]['settings']['index'], though the index state (open/closed) is at cluster.state['metadata']['indices'][INDEXNAME]['state']

Any program that uses getopt_long (which is more or less the de facto standard) or emulates the same behavior will allow for partial name matching. This includes the standard Python argument parser (argparse) which we are using, and thus got this for "free".

We had a lively discussion on the pros and cons of having this feature (it can be turned off in Python 3.5+ I believe - would have to look up the specific version that it was introduced) but there is no supported way of doing so in argparse in earlier versions.

Since both argparse and getopt_long have this behavior, and both are very widely used, the current assumption is that the existing behavior is not completely unexpected to users.

For what it's worth, Personally, I now frequently take advantage of this feature after having used the CLI for a while, and would be bummed out if it was removed :)

We are obviously open to suggestions to change this behavior, but I would suggest that we gather additional data before making an effort to change this.

The string is OS version string which is given by Ubuntu. We do not have the control over the format of the string.
``` Python
# Windows
platform.version()
'10.0.15063'

# MacOS
platform.version()
Darwin Kernel Version 16.6.0: Fri Apr 14 16:21:16 PDT 2017; root:xnu-3789.60.24-6/RELEASE_X86_X64

# Ubuntu
platform.version()
#100-ubuntu smp wed may 17 19:58:14 utc 2017
```

We return system type in OS.Type, which is 'Windows', 'Linux', and 'Darwin'. There isn't a good way to generalize the different distro among Linux without extensive string parsing.

The role assignments configure access to azure assets for principals managed in directory graph. The integration is very tight, particularly the rbac service only accepts graph object_id, so for any logic name (unp, spn, group name, etc), commands will resolve to object_ids before submit the request. Even for xplat, the `create` command exposes more common flags to take logical name, and the same with `delete`, and `list`.  For xplat, through object_id you can by-pass the graph access for that step, but there is no end-to-end story though, because you have no way to query for the right SP based on logical names unless you remember the guid, which is hard. 
Of course if it becomes a mainline that SP should be able to create assignments, we can update cli, but so far i have not got user data to justify it.

@avaranovich the second scenario is not actually supported. The other user must be a member of the same AAD as the data lake store account in order to have any access. So you would need to log in as that user using the tenant ID of the AAD tenant the Data Lake Store account is in. 

For ADLS, I recommend going through the following steps with your account through the portal first to make sure they work:
1. identify the user/tenant combination of an owner of the ADLS account
2. verify that, as the owner you can access any file/folder that was created by a different user (this is the "super user" feature, where members of the account owner group will always have full access to all data in the account). If you still get access denied errors, then the user you are logged in with may not actually be a member of the owner group for the resource. Try going back to the IAM blade in the portal for the ADLS account and adding this user to the owner group

Now, with your service principal, or a different account:
3. login through python using the same tenant ID as the one your ADLS account was created under. This is very important, since cross tenant auth is not supported for ADLS.
4. Create your file/folder as the service principal/other user. If this fails with permissions issues, make sure to give this account/service principal write and execute permissions at a minimum on the folder structure leading to where the user wants to write to.

Now that the service principal (or other user) created a file, you can go back to the portal as your owner account and try to access it. If this still fails, double check again that the owner is logged in to the right AAD tenant and that it is a member of the owner group. If all of this is true and you are still reproducing this issue please give me both the script and the repro steps (without your sensitive information) and I will investigate with our security and access team to help unblock you.

@adamn: it seems you assume there that the only way in which Python 2.6 becomes a burden for `pre-commit` is when tests break on it. In addition to untested failure modes that aren't caught that way, being Python 2.6 compatible also has an impact on the code and tooling (e.g., `pip` availability) itself in the first place. Breaking loose from Python 2.6 could be an advantage. So, I would say we should take a broader perspective on the ups and downs of keeping Python 2.6 compat.

I would like to add to your point about living code bases, that projects can just keep using older versions of pre-commit that are compatible. I.e., support for platforms for legacy reasons shouldn't really be an argument here. Only if Python 2.6-based projects were still expecting to use new features in precommit this would be an issue, but as you wrote, it is a dead platform.

I'm not sure how your project is set up, but assuming you have the Python 3 files separate from the Python 2 files, what if you ran linters (in this example, `flake8`) on the files separately?

```yaml
repos:
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git
    sha: v0.9.1
    hooks:
    -   id: flake8
        name: flake8 (Python 2)
        # Adjust as necessary
        files: ^py2/
        language_version: python2.7
    -   id: flake8
        name: flake8 (Python 3)
        # Adjust as necessary
        files: ^py3/
        language_version: python3.6
```

There's also the `exclude` key which you could use instead.

Okay I think I made some progress with conversion... loading things up in gazebo isn't exactly a walk in the park it seems they need things to be in a very specific layout which I detail below.

```
└── simple_box
    ├── model.config
    ├── simple_box.dae
    ├── simple_box.sdf
    └── simple_box.urdf

```

The following gazebo command will convert files, provide warnings about what is wrong, and print a converted file out on the command line: 

```bash
gz-8.1.1 sdf -p ~/src/bullet3/data/kuka_iiwa/kuka_with_gripper.sdf
```

The printout which can be redirected to overwrite the original sdf file, for example:

```bash
gz-8.1.1 sdf -p ~/src/bullet3/data/kuka_iiwa/kuka_with_gripper.sdf >  ~/src/bullet3/data/kuka_iiwa/kuka_with_gripper.sdf 
```

### Models

Here are some very simple models I've compressed for convenience, plus an updated `simple_box.zip`, these all load in both gazebo and V-REP except V-REP doesn't seem to support textures:
- [simple_box.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1053603/simple_box.zip) your simple_box updated to load in gazebo and V-REP
- [ball_bearing.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1053568/ball_bearing.zip) should be dark gray, has a material definition (a V-REP option), and is otherwise very simple
- [bookshelf.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1053569/bookshelf.zip) includes a texture and is composed of several simple boxes
- [cinder_block.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1053606/cinder_block.zip) includes a collada mesh

The materials spec they have also seems to be important if there is to be a shelf made of wood:
```

        <material>
          <script>
            <uri>file://media/materials/scripts/gazebo.material</uri>
            <name>Gazebo/Wood</name>
          </script>
        </material>
```

And gazebo won't load model/mesh files next to the sdf file unless it is specified with `model://model_folder_name/model_mesh_file.dae`, as you can see in this working version of `simple_box.dae`:

```xml
<sdf version='1.6'>
  <model name='cube'>
    <link name='baseLink'>
      <pose frame=''>0 0 0 0 -0 0</pose>
      <inertial>
        <pose frame=''>0 0 0 0 -0 0</pose>
        <mass>1</mass>
        <inertia>
          <ixx>1</ixx>
          <ixy>0</ixy>
          <ixz>0</ixz>
          <iyy>1</iyy>
          <iyz>0</iyz>
          <izz>1</izz>
        </inertia>
      </inertial>
      <collision name='baseLink_collision'>
        <pose frame=''>0 0 0 0 -0 0</pose>
        <geometry>
          <box>
            <size>1 1 1</size>
          </box>
        </geometry>
      </collision>
      <visual name='baseLink_visual'>
        <pose frame=''>0 0 0 0 -0 0</pose>
        <geometry>
          <mesh>
            <scale>1 1 1</scale>
            <uri>model://simple_box/simple_box.dae</uri>
          </mesh>
        </geometry>
      </visual>
    </link>
  </model>
</sdf>
```

It seems part of the solution is that the models don't need to define the `world`, they can simply be a `model` like the files above. An additional requirement for gazebo is the `model.config` file, which specifies the name of the object and the path to the sdf file. Gazebo also requires model file (like collada).

### Simulations

The `world` definition is what has all those extra requirements such as specifying gravity, light sources, simulation refresh rate, etc. You may want to support simulation definitions, but it should likely not be used for simple model definitions.

Here is everything needed for a basic simulation world definition but note that there seems to be a [small bug in the saved vs loaded dimensions as specified in gazebo 8.1.1](https://bitbucket.org/osrf/gazebo/issues/2292/world-does-not-save-and-load-consistently).

- [brick_box_world.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/1053693/brick_box_world.zip) A world containing default settings and a brick box

It sounds like there is a problem for some people, but it's not clear under what conditions it occurs.

The behaviour I'd expect in a terminal is:

1. Ctrl-C is delivered to the terminal, where it will typically be translated into SIGINT.
2. If the frontend (terminal emulator) is in Linux or Windows, Ctrl-Shift-C copies to the clipboard.
3. Other platform copy shortcuts, such as Cmd-C on OS X and Ctrl-Insert on Linux, should work as normal.

Browser limitations may prevent us from implementing 2, because we can only access the clipboard when the browser's own mechanisms handle a clipboard shortcut. But it sounds like there are some problems with 1.

This is probably an issue with xterm.js, and it may already be fixed, either in a version we've already bundled into Jupyter since it was reported, or a still newer version. But without a reliable way to reproduce it, we don't really know.

- Follow http://cssguidelin.es/#bem-like-naming for css classes.
- IDs are uppercase and prefixed with ``ID_``. Example ``ID_MAINMENU``. IDs are **never** used for styling, but they may be used for automatic tests.

> I do think we can eventually deprecate the use of passing an iterable of types to `PolyModelType` and move those use cases to `UnionType`, i.e. write `UnionType([TruckType(), BoatType()])` instead of `PolyModelType([TruckType(), BoatType()])`.

`PolyType/UnionType` takes an iterable of *types*, while `PolyModelType` is already a variant of `ModelType` that takes an iterable of *models*.

```PolyModelType([A, B, C])```

is equivalent to

```PolyType([ModelType(A), ModelType(B), ModelType(C)])```.

Okay -- the EREMOTEIO error was causing the 500 response, however, as @jcnelson mentions, there's an underlying failure which causes the REMOTEIO error response (which is itself an error path).

The underlying failure has to do with the `client.ini` setting:

```
storage_drivers_required_write = disk,dropbox
```

That's the default setting in production, and the default setting in regtest is:
```
storage_drivers_required_write = disk
```

Switching production to `storage_drivers_required_write=disk` _should_ solve this issue, am trying it with that hotfix branch now.

> Specifies, which of the standard I/O files (stdin, stdout and stderr) are to be transferred to/from the execution host. If not set, defaults to "". Any combination of 'e', 'i' and 'o' may be specified. See drmaa_input_path, drmaa_output_path and drmaa_error_path for information about how to specify the standard input file, standard output file and standard error file. The file transfer mechanism itself must be configured by the administrator (see sge_conf(5) ). When it is configured, the administrator has to enable drmaa_transfer_files. It's a little complicated because I want a user var to return the string `"undefined"` when called in RiveScript code, e.g. `<get name>` should return "undefined" if that variable isn't set for the user. But, from your Python code, functions like `get_uservar()`, there should be a way to differentiate whether the user is *missing* the variable entirely, or the variable was literally set to the word `"undefined"`. (Note that `get_uservar()` currently does return `"undefined"` when the variable doesn't exist, but that should probably be fixed as well).

The problem started when I moved the session management code into its own module, where the same logic was being called both for `<get>` tags as well as the API functions. The session manager is what returns the string `"undefined"` when a variable doesn't exist, and the API functions can't tell whether the variable *really* doesn't exist or not.

Probably the best place to fix it would be in the session manager [around here](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/blob/4d865fd5a03ec0b1a6ca321d10d60582496573e0/rivescript/sessions.py#L197). The function could accept a new `default` parameter (to set your own "default value" when the variable doesn't exist), and do something like:

```python
    def get(self, username, key, default="undefined"):
        if not username in self._users:
            return None
        return self._users[username].get(key, default)
```

This will preserve the existing behavior when a `<get>` tag is called, because the code won't provide a `default` parameter and therefore use the word `"undefined"` by default. But then the functions like `get_uservar()` and `last_match()` can be updated to set `default=None`.

Changing the [`rivescript.rivescript.get_uservar()`](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/blob/4d865fd5a03ec0b1a6ca321d10d60582496573e0/rivescript/rivescript.py#L826) function to do this should fix both things at the same time:

```diff
- return self._session.get(user, name)
+ return self._session.get(user, name, None)
```

The `last_match()` function does `return self.get_uservar(user, "__lastmatch__")` which would automatically benefit from the code change to `get_uservar()`.

-----

On an unrelated side note on deleting user vars: some people have a need to delete temporary user variables once they're done with them. Calling `rs.set_uservar(user, "name", None)` makes it delete that variable from memory altogether rather than just set its value to `"undefined"`.

It's supposed to also be the case that you can do `<set name=undefined>` in RiveScript code and it should delete the variable too, but I don't think that currently happens. You end up with the variable `name` containing the literal string `"undefined"`, which works for all intents and purposes (i.e. comparisons checking if `<get name> == undefined` work as expected), but under the hood the variable wasn't technically deleted.

@maruqu @jgoclawski This is a bit more extensive/invasive in terms of changes. Given the heavy reliance on `raw_object` as the source of truth and dynamic access to it, my approach was thus:

* Create a `dict` on the model to hold cached deserialized values to prevent deserializing a field every time it's read
* Update the map opportunistically on first read
* Update the map on set
* Clear the map if `raw_object` itself is replaced

My original approach stored the cached value on the `AttributeField` itself, but this caused problems when refreshed. Since the model actually has no concept of what fields it contains, clearing these out after a refresh would mean reflecting on the object and looking for any `AttributeField` fields and wiping it out. Hence, the singular `dict` on the model itself.

I'm not crazy about swapping `raw_object` to a property but it was the cleanest way I could find so that I could hook into the setter -- there's not a consistent "update" path besides setting this value (since again, it's really the source of truth for the model).

Anyway, I'm open to feedback and making changes. Hopefully you don't mind my little parade of PRs! 😁

so first of all I was able to do this on my system without issues:

```
In [2]: p = angr.Project('vmlinuz')

In [3]: p
Out[3]: <Project vmlinuz>

In [4]: p.loader
Out[4]: <Loaded vmlinuz, maps [0x0:0x2008000]>
```

second of all, angr's notion of the address space is nothing less than the full 2^32 or 2^64 byte addressable space. If you run out you've run out and there's absolutely nothing that can be done. However angr does have some quirky ideas about what constitutes a legal filling of the address space, notably:

- Nothing may be loaded at a higher address than the main binary.
- Objects must be loaded at a pretty strict granularity, currently 2^24 by default. This can be tweaked in the constructor.
- There must be room for a few additional objects which also obey these rules, if they are applicable: an externs object to point unresolved relocations and misc address allocation requirements (angr will almost always try to pull this in), and an object to allocate addresses for syscalls (angr will pull this in if it thinks the binary has the ability to make system calls)

You can see in the traceback that it's actually finished the normal loading just fine, but it's failing to pull in the externs object, presumably because your kernel is position-dependent and mapped at the top of the address space. You'd have to hack into the source to fix this, though tbh it should probably just be an option.

Since it's a position-independent executable, the CLE loads it at some base address, 0x400000 by default. First, you need to extract the addresses and then add it to the base address. Try this:
```
for x in symbols_by_addr:
    symbol = symbols_by_addr[x]
    addr = proj.entry + symbol.addr
    name = symbol.name
    print "%s: %s" % (hex(addr), name)
    entry_points.append((addr, name))
```

So, let me try to sum it up: the idea is to make the necessary changes so that `record:create` would implicitly allow to read the collection object.

In terms of specs (and thus [unit tests to implement](http://blog.mathieu-leplatre.info/your-tests-as-your-specs.html)), it becomes:

If Alice has no permission to read the collection but has `record:create`
* she can read the collection object
* she obtains a 403 if she created no record in that collection (*current behaviour*)
* she can obtain the list of her own records (*current behaviour*)
* records of other users do not come up in the list  (*current behaviour*)
* she cannot read individual records of other users  (*current behaviour*)

If Alice has the permission to read the collection, she can read the collection object, and every records, but cannot create records (*current behaviour*).

This is a little tricky because PyWavelets does not currently have an equivalent of the `wrcoef` function from Matlab.  An equivalent to that function can be implemented for 1D data by manually looping over calls to `pywt.upcoef`.  In the example below, `a` should end up equivalent to `A` in your Matlab code above: 

```python
import numpy as np
import pywt
data = np.arange(255.)
level = 5
c = pywt.wavedec(data, 'db4', level=level)

a, details = c[0], c[1:]
# size at each reconstruction level
L = [d.size for d in details[1:]] + [data.size, ]

for n in range(level):
    a = pywt.upcoef('a', a, 'db4', level=1, take=L[n])
```

You should look into it.  From what you described I would say that there are two possibilities of what's happening here:

1. When you press the "Create Session" button the second time, the first create-session action is interrupted, and it tries to create a second session with the same name.  This case is probably resolvable on our end, we'll just have to do some digging.  (Note that generally we don't design sparkcontroller methods as if they can be interrupted, so we might want to review the code again to make sure there are no other similar race conditions.)

2.  When you press the "Create Session" button the second time, it runs it concurrently along with the first request.  If this is the case, then we should do some queuing or something on the widgets side to make sure that only one thread is mutating the sparkcontroller at a time.

In scikit-learn this can be done using the feature_selection module. Feature selection is often integrated into the pipeline as it can depend on the training of the final estimator. Usually in scikit-learn it is part of the pre-processing steps. With methods like feature elimination, it is not known what features are important until after the model is trained. It is also common to do implicit basis expansion (kernel methods) where you get this for free. 

From a ML perspective, it feels like you would only explicitly add a function feature if you had a prior reason for believing it was important. If you were just trying to find better performance through basis expansion, that could be done using scikit-learn as part of the training pipeline, or by using a kernel method. The scikit-learn methods could be integrated here, but is adding this within the scope of matminer?

The `_CommonTestMixin` provides methods very similar to what is already present elsewhere in Pulp Smash. For example:

* `test_status_code` is similar to [`pulp_smash.tests.rpm.api_v2.test_sync_publish.PublishTestCase.test_status_code`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.html#pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.PublishTestCase.test_status_code).
* `test_task_error_traceback` is similar to [`pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.SyncInvalidFeedTestCase.test_task_error_traceback`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.html#pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.SyncInvalidFeedTestCase.test_task_error_traceback) and [`pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.SyncValidFeedTestCase.test_task_error_traceback`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.html#pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.SyncValidFeedTestCase.test_task_error_traceback).

More generally, the `RemoveMissingTrueTestCase` and `RemoveMissingFalseTestCase` act much like the existing sync and publish test cases. Each of these test cases performs actions like syncing, adding distributors, and adding publishers, and then records the responses from pulp and makes assertions about those responses.

In a way, it's nice to have these tests. More tests means better constraints on Pulp's behaviour, right? However, these new tests are largely redundant: module [`pulp_smash.tests.rpm.api_v2.test_sync_publish`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.tests.puppet.api_v2.test_sync_publish.html) already provides assurance that Pulp is capable of creating RPM repositories and performing syncs. And redundant test code has several costs:

* Adding these tests will extend the amount of time it takes for Pulp Smash to execute. (It already takes at least ten minutes to execute the test suite on a pair of 2.7 and 2.8 systems on my development system.) A long test suite execution time is bad because it extends the feedback loop experienced by developers and other users. We want people to use Pulp Smash, not say "eh, I don't want to wait 30 minutes to see if my changes are OK."
* Adding these tests will increase the maintenance burden. If Pulp changes the format of its task reports, or if Pulp changes the status codes it returns, or anything else, it should be easy for us to update Pulp Smash accordingly. @Feryal this is a known issue where GLFW rendering isn't supported on Linux. It's currently using OSMesa, which is unfortunately much slower (but makes headless rendering easier since you don't need xserver running etc). That said, it should be pretty straight forward to add GLFW support by just changing [`builder.py`](https://github.com/openai/mujoco-py/blob/master/mujoco_py/builder.py#L157) to link against GLFW on Linux as well, and then add some flag to `MjSim.render` to make it use the GLFW backend instead. docu here: http://botocore.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/cloudformation.html#CloudFormation.Client.delete_stack
for convenience I copied the two relevant sections here:

## RetainResources
For stacks in the DELETE_FAILED state, a list of resource logical IDs that are associated with the resources you want to retain. During deletion, AWS CloudFormation deletes the stack but does not delete the retained resources.

Retaining resources is useful when you cannot delete a resource, such as a non-empty S3 bucket, but you want to delete the stack.

## RoleARN
The Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of an AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) role that AWS CloudFormation assumes to delete the stack. AWS CloudFormation uses the role's credentials to make calls on your behalf.

If you don't specify a value, AWS CloudFormation uses the role that was previously associated with the stack. If no role is available, AWS CloudFormation uses a temporary session that is generated from your user credentials.

Okay after quick review of UPC-A
escpos spec says you can supply 11 or 12 character data.

UPC-A spec requires 12 digits.
if 11 digits my printer calculates the check digit. 
if 12 digits my printer prints the supplied 12th character check digit.
this is the best of both worlds I'd say.

a very quick look at code 128 with both my smartphone and usb scanner seems to indicate the checksum is calculated

Considering all the barcodes in my test scanned I would say the printer is supposed to handle the checksum for us.

So my conclusion is if a user wants to supply their own checksum they simply pass it, otherwise it is calculated by the printer.
That said, maybe non-epson stuff doesnt work this way?
Regardless we shouldn't write escpos to a specific printer but keep it generic.

Hi @msobroza,

One way to get context vectors from a pre-trained model is to use the `dump` subcommand:
```
> fasttext dump model.bin output
```
will print the weights of the "context matrix", while
```
> fasttext dump model.bin dict
```
will print the vocabulary of the model.

Please note that for the models trained on common crawl with position weights (https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/crawl-vectors.html), the same vectors were used for context and target.

Best,
Edouard

I like the proposal as well. 👍 

Most other libraries (like Plumbum.colors) just use colorama to do the windows API conversion (by patching sys.stdout, IIRC). Due to the drastically different nature of the API calls and escape sequences, it's the only reasonable way to do it.

The Plumbum.colors code for setting the correct color defaults on common platforms is here if that helps:

https://github.com/tomerfiliba/plumbum/blob/22db2e82b114c02592716530241448bb66b76fdf/plumbum/colorlib/styles.py#L43-L60

Plumbum.colors does strip colors by default, but you can override that by setting the color settings (or by using the HTML output). Putting ansi escape sequences in a log file used to be frowned on as a default, though colors are not too bad. (Cursor movement, on the other hand, is probably always a bad idea).

There are a couple more potentially desirable changes of a similar nature.  

First, the **run** command simply re-runs a command from the history.  We can probably added a **--run** argument to the **history** command which encompasses the same functionality.

Secondly, the **edit** command is a bit odd in its behavior where if it is passed an integer it edits that command number from the history (and runs the edited version when it exits), whereas if it is passed something that isn't an integer it edits that file.  It would probably make more logical sense to add a **--edit** argument to the **history** command that encompasses the functionality of editing and running a previous command and then have the **edit** command simply edit files.

I think these would be good changes to make.  They could be worked under this issue since they are all similar in nature, or we could create separate issues for them.

What pybind with the factory PR is essentially:

- When the new type is created, a new pointer is is allocated with `T::operator new(sizeof(T))` and stored in the part of the python object that pybind manages.
- When the factory init is called, it gets a new pointer, calls `T::operator delete(oldptr);` on the existing pointer in the object, then stores the new pointer in the object instead.

Ah, I see the issue: your class doesn't *have* `T::operator delete(oldptr)`, and we aren't handling that.

> Yes -- but what if we're compiling code on a c++17 compilant compiler that was still using std::experimental::optional?

At least for libstdc++, this is impossible: including `<optional>` in C++14 or below is an (intentional) compilation error, and including `<experimental/optional>` in C++17 is likewise a compilation error.

I took a look at clang with libc++, and, at least as of llvm 3.9,  the current `c++1z` support doesn't have `<optional>`, so I can't really say what will happen there.

I now added a brief introduction to the ``docstring_options`` class to the **Generating documentation using Sphinx** section of the manual.

I also changed the design of the class a bit. I didn't like the initialization constructor, which took two boolean flags to control the suppression of user-defined docstrings and auto-generated signatures. This is prone to errors and hard to remember. Explicit method calls are now necessary to configure the docstring generation (which is more verbose but easier to remember):

```
py::docstring_options doc_options;
doc_options.disable_signatures();
...
```

Also extended the test case to fully cover docstring_options' functionality.

> Is it fine if the `BUILD_INTERFACE` is conditional?

I would very much like to avoid that if at all possible. Even if the config file can't `find_package(pybind11)`, it should still be able to do the equivalent by including the [relevant lines](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/cca20a7f8d980dd9de2c1054076a7da6b51b5b5d/tools/pybind11Config.cmake.in#L86-L96) to populate the Python-specific properties.

Hello Pim,

pybind11 has no support for detecting/preserving the ``const``ness of values (which has no equivalent in Python, so it's actually a quite alien concept to force on Python). It's the sort of thing which is theoretically feasible, but would significantly complicate the library. I decided to draw the line on the side of simplicity in this case.

Best,
Wenzel

The second `__init__` indeed looks weird; and moreover I don't think it'll ever actually be invoked.  It was added in 6fb48490 along with other changes to fix a problem, but I don't think the second constructor was actually the fix here.  (I also don't think the second `__init__` will ever be called, since pybind looks for things to call based on their signatures, and these have identical signatures).

The non-deleted pointer is intentional: registration of classes/enums is permanent, and that pointer needs to be alive (because `__repr__` uses it) as long as the enum is registered, i.e. forever.  The `enum_` instance itself, however, only persists during the initialization code: but its effects, which depend on that unordered map, remain forever.

Certbot doesn't have Solaris support! So this reply is a guide to building it for yourself, if you'd like to :)

```
./certbot-auto: syntax error at line 23: `BASENAME=$' unexpected
```

That's odd -- it seems your system does not have a POSIX compliant `/bin/sh`:

http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/007904875/utilities/xcu_chap02.html#tag_02_02_03
http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695299/utilities/basename.html

You may be up against [this issue](https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/164203/will-a-shell-script-execute-in-solaris-if-not-which-is-the-fastest-way-to-make), which honestly makes me think Solaris is going to be insane and hard to support without breaking compatibility with other things.  You could just edit your copy of `certbot-auto` to use a POSIX-y shell, and then always run with `--no-self-upgrade`.

But once you've solved that, you're still going to have the problem that `certbot-auto` doesn't currently have an OS bootstrapper to provide Solaris support, so you'll have to figure out how to get Certbot's dependencies installed yourself. A workaround would be to follow [these instructions](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#running-a-local-copy-of-the-client) to run Certbot without `certbot-auto` (but instead of the `--os-packages-only` step, install OS dependencies, which are goups 1,2,3,4 in [this list](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1301#issue-114753911) manually).

Or you could even send us a PR that adds a Solaris [bootstrapper](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/bootstrappers) to `certbot-auto`.

Nextly, if you want to run the [Apache plugin](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#plugins), you'll need to make sure that it knows the correct values for all of these commands and directories on a Solaris system: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/constants.py.  You could do that by providing lots of flags like `--apache-vhost-root` etc setting values for these, if the defaults are wrong, or again send us a PR adding them to that `constants.py` file.

Reducing the duplicated code between Lexicon-based plugins definitely seems like a good idea. It would definitely reduce the maintenance burden in this area, including the effort around code reviews — which I'm far too slow at.

I know that this is something we looked at last year. Unfortunately, I don't recall all of the reasons we didn't want to go this route.

I think we should be able to address the problems in this space (duplicated code, high cost of adding and reviewing new plugins, etc.), but I'm not sure the proposed implementation (a single plugin) is the best way to do so. (It may well be the best way to do so, but as with many things I think it's worth starting with the problems, considering possible solutions, and then comparing those solutions to pick the best one.)

Some things to consider:
- From the point of view of an end-user, Lexicon is an implementation detail. A typical user may know what DNS provider they use, but almost certainly doesn't know whether Lexicon supports that provider (and if Lexicon supports the provider, whether that's how Certbot has chosen to implement support for the provider — an early design principle for the DNS plugins was to prefer first-party libraries to Lexicon when available).
   - To me, this suggests that support for providers A, B, and C should _look_ the same even if A and B are supported via Lexicon and C is not.
- As mentioned above, there are differences between DNS providers which are not handled by Lexicon. The most notable area is error handling; almost every provider has a different set of errors (expressed via HTTP status or specific error strings) that indicate different mistakes a user may have made. Other differences exist in the areas of propagation delays and types of authentication information expected.
- We don't currently have a way to conduct end-to-end testing of plugins. Last I looked, Lexicon didn't either. The more tightly coupled the support for a set of providers is, the harder it is to make changes. (Currently, introducing one plugin doesn't risk breaking an existing one, but if introducing support for a new provider required updating a library used for all providers, it might be necessary to manually test all existing providers to ensure that nothing broke.)
- In addition to code-level duplication between plugins, there's also duplication around documentation. Perhaps this can/should be addressed in a broader way.
- In addition to code- and documentation-related duplication, there's also a fair bit of boilerplate to add a new plugin to the project. Perhaps this can/should be addressed in a broader way.
- Most typical users only have a single DNS provider. (I don't remember exactly why this is relevant, but it's definitely something that was pointed out previously... I think the context was essentially that all else being equal we should put the users' experience ahead of ours.)

It's been decided that issue should be split up into smaller issues. Quite some time has been put in to this issue already, but due to some difficulties it has not been finalized yet.

The current process of this issue is the following:

* Updated smart contracts to use mappings instead of arrays for keeping track of the open channels. (This means that we lose the possibility to retrieve all open channels by iterating through the array, but we should still be able to build the list of all open channels by querying events from the blockchain.) The smart contract tests are passing from this change.

* After above changes the call for getting all channels (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/250/commits/e20a0cd69b067e22f965b9a19f21ed0648347c44#diff-c53a383092e17d1404bc7405c9568185L425) was no longer available. Therefore it was necessary to implement functionality for iterating through all events on the BC in order to build the list of open channels. This was attempted to be solved with this commit (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/250/commits/a530f723c04f297eebeb554e6ef60727556a9c7a), but it seems that the problem is that `tester_client.py` is using some genesis block that does not omit events, and therefore nothing is added to the list of open channels. This leads to a lot of failing tests. And since parts of the code depends on the `assetAddresses()` function of the smart contracts, we cannot just swap changes to the smart contracts. 

* It's also been suggested that we access the storage of the contract directly to get the values from the mapping, but this turned out to not be possible either, since the values of the mappings are only stored as hashes.

* Going forward from here we need a way to ensure that events are omitted when using `tester_client.py`, so that we can build the list of open channels. @hackaugusto recently suggested that it might be worth looking into just returning here (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/tests/fixtures/blockchain.py#L85), since that would mean that no genesis block is used.

@robertnishihara Job refers to the one discussed in this [PRD](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1yPTn6kMwIjdo_dpFBqb0_FwtNVCXEaiSwKWaLyQmUl4/edit#). Yes there's a one-to-one mapping between _job_ and _driver_, on the other hand, _job_ concept has a broader scope than a _driver_. Generally, job refers to all the tasks (directly or indirectly invoked) by a driver. 

There are a couple of differences:
- When user submits a job, he could provide job name, code files (jars or python scripts), resource requirement, priorities .etc, these all refers to the whole job instead of the driver itself.
- When the driver terminates, current raylet behaviors is other actors would continue to run, and the job would only finish until all the tasks for this job complete. 
- When user kill a job, or get a job status, this also refers to all the tasks for this job.

A couple of things:

1. it looks like there is an extra left margin in your screenshot. Is the output widget not deleting that left margin?
2. The background thread issue is a bit more complicated - threaded output in the ipython kernel isn't cleanly supported, since it's not just scoped to a thread, but also scoped to the execution in the main thread. In other words, if you start a background thread, the background thread output goes into whatever output the main thread happens to be sending things to. What the output widget does is offer a way to redirect that background threaded output to somewhere we choose. We can't rely on the display() mechanism for this since it suffers the problem above. To get around this, we append directly to the output widget, bypassing the ipython display mechanism.

Funnily enough, it appears that implicit exception chaining is actually doing what we want here; there's some discussion at https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5ad5753a27c509a7741cca44.

```
In [4]: async def afn():
   ...:     try:
   ...:         with trio.fail_after(1):
   ...:             await trio.sleep(5)
   ...:     except trio.TooSlowError as exc:
   ...:         return exc
   ...:

In [5]: exc = trio.run(afn)

In [6]: exc
Out[6]: trio.TooSlowError()

In [7]: exc.__context__
Out[7]: trio.Cancelled()
```
Simpler illustrative example:
```
In [8]: class suppress:
   ...:     def __enter__(self):
   ...:         pass
   ...:     def __exit__(self, *exc_info):
   ...:         return True
   ...:

In [9]: from contextlib import contextmanager

In [10]: @contextmanager
    ...: def chain():
    ...:     with suppress():
    ...:         yield
    ...:     raise ValueError
    ...:

In [11]: with chain():
    ...:     raise KeyError
    ...:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-10-bf8693b31045> in chain()
      3     with suppress():
----> 4         yield
      5     raise ValueError

<ipython-input-11-6f32b66051a3> in <module>()
      1 with chain():
----> 2     raise KeyError

KeyError:

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-11-6f32b66051a3> in <module>()
      1 with chain():
----> 2     raise KeyError

/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.5/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/contextlib.py in __exit__(self, type, value, traceback)
     97                 value = type()
     98             try:
---> 99                 self.gen.throw(type, value, traceback)
    100             except StopIteration as exc:
    101                 # Suppress StopIteration *unless* it's the same exception that

<ipython-input-10-bf8693b31045> in chain()
      3     with suppress():
      4         yield
----> 5     raise ValueError

ValueError:
```
This is pretty subtle, though, so probably we still want the explicit chaining or a comment in fail_after. It's also unlikely that the implicit chaining will do the right thing with MultiErrors; my guess would be that the entire MultiError gets attached rather than just the Cancelled part, but I haven't verified this.

                                    content_type='application/octet-stream',
                                    HTTP_X_REQUESTED_WITH='XMLHttpRequest')
        uploaded_file_path = os.path.join(settings.MEDIA_ROOT, 'InstitutionImages',

class AjaxFileUploaderFork(AjaxFileUploader):

    def _ajax_upload(self, request):
        if request.method == "POST":
            if request.is_ajax():
                upload = request
                is_raw = True
                try:
                    filename = request.GET['qqfile']
                except KeyError:
                    return HttpResponseBadRequest("AJAX request not valid")
            else:
                is_raw = False
                if len(request.FILES) == 1:
                    upload = request.FILES.values()[0]
                else:
                    raise Http404("Bad Upload")
                filename = upload.name

            backend = self.get_backend()
            institution = get_institution_moderation(request)
            if not institution:
                orig_inst = Institution.objects.get(id=int(request.GET.get(
                    'institution')))
                institution = add_moderation_class(orig_inst)
            backend.UPLOAD_DIR = 'InstitutionImages/%d' % institution.id

            filename, url = backend.setup(filename)
            success = backend.upload(upload, filename, is_raw)

            extra_context = backend.upload_complete(request, filename)
            photo = InstitutionPhoto()
            photo.image.save(filename, ImageFile(open(url, 'r')), save=True)
            photo.name = filename
            photo.save()
            institution.photos.add(photo)
            institution = set_moderation_status_pending(institution)
            thumbnail_options = dict(size=(100, 100), crop=True, bw=True,
                                     upscale=True)
            thumbnail = get_thumbnailer(photo.image).get_thumbnail(
                thumbnail_options)

            ret_json = {'success': success, 'filename': filename,
                        'thumbnail': thumbnail.url}
            if extra_context is not None:
                ret_json.update(extra_context)

            return HttpResponse(json.dumps(ret_json))
